author: @58lfes
text: Excellent idea! Could you not simply fill a transparent tube with a fluorescent liquid? I would imagine the light would concentrate at the ends of the tube since it'll act like a large optical fibre. I'd have thought it would be easier to build than a flat panel system; plus you can use most of the area of a standard solar cell, you'd just need a tube with the appropriate radius. Essentially It can go up instead of flat, like a solar tree instead of a panel.

author: @Hybris71
text: If you're using the gel with reflective paper behind it, you must compare the result to using just the reflective paper, not using nothing at all. Otherwise, how do you know it's not the simple reflection from the paper that's causing the increase in voltage from the solar cell? My money is on the paper causing at least 80% of it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's more likely the gel triangle is acting as a prism - a fairer test would be to use a prism with a reflector

author: @taylorwestmore4664
text: Now you've got me thinking, the fluorescent plastic sheets would be  the perfect substrate for Graphene ink based solar cells. Alternating layers of P-type and N-type semiconductor doped reduced Graphene inks could have a broadly tuneable bandgap, just by controlling the thickness of the layers or the thickness of undoped graphene separator layers. This could be achieved for large surface areas by controlling the molarity of Graphene water solution and using a fixed quantity of water for a given plastic surface area. This way you can lay down arbitrarily small layers of a known mass of Graphene, ideally down to the hundreds of nano-meters range needed for ultraviolet to infrared light. To derive the mass required for a given thickness of layer deposition you must use the values of thickness of your Graphene (single layer, multi-layer etc), the surface area of Graphene per unit mass, and the surface area of the sheet. These numbers will allow you to solve for the mass needed for any number of layers of Graphene, then you take that mass and disperse it in a fixed volume of water, ideally just enough ink will be produced to cover your desired surface area in a thin layer of Graphene water solution, which can then evaporate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: now you have me thinking mate!

author: @johnschneider931
text: How the hell do you get deionized water. I have only seen that in my dads blood testing lab.😂

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Water can be deionised following the application of Reverse Osmosis (RO). RO is where water is passed through a semi-permeable membrane to remove water impurities. This includes ions, organic compounds and particulates. Water processed using RO is already attaining a pure at the end.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they sell it in auto repair stores mate

author: @jamest.5001
text:
Maybe take the Florescent ink and make a sprayabl dye from it and make a fresnel lense  Florescent , and lay it over a solar panel! I have a 20 watt panel, maybe I will give it a try eventually, it would be awesome to double the output from the panel, only 20watts, especially on cloudy days, I use it to maintain batteries! They already have a coating on the glass, maybe they can put a Florescent coating on it as well,  but I don't think they want to make them too much more efficient, too fast, to keep from upsetting the nature of things, disrupt  markets, 
Imagine if they came up with a panel that was 99% efficient,  collecting 1000watts per sq meter, then 10 watt per sq cm, or a 1x3 meter panel that output 2500watts, and cost the same as a 250-300 watt panel produced 5years ago, say 2500 watts for $200 or less,  and did 60% of rated power on cloudy days, last 50 years loosing 25% capacity or less in that time! Power companies would be shutting down plants , fuel suppliers would go bankrupt,  especially if battery tech get a similar boost in performance, everyone could power their home from solar, store enough energy to power their home for a week with no sun, and output 2kw to the grid,  if every house did that, the less solar friendly places could use the grid,  that is supported by the 2kw from 70% of homes, and 90% of Business and government buildings that also power them selves,  with solar farms powering the grid, everyone buying and selling power, but mostly selling, getting a power payment, instead of a bill monthly,  around $100 average,  the factories and office buildings in cities would buy the most power, that supports the grid,  and powering cities, houses in the country, outside of the cities would supply most of the power to cities, solar farms with batteries, with 2-4, 40kw 3phase inverters, with 400-500kwh battery each, run about 80% power, supplying power 24/7,   with homes supplying 2kw +, 24/7,  with a smart breaker system that can shut off power when not in use, to prevent vampire loads, and  light switches that know if you are in the room,  every thing energy efficient, and smart enough to know you are not home to use power, slot of this is possible now, have  breakers and outlets,.light switches, that can tell when you are home , in a room, shut off things when you are not home, like the refrigerator, it could be shut off part of the day, after running a slight longer run to cool colder, to near freezing when you are at work, and not run until it's 41°F  when you are about to come home, maybe there can be a phone app, that reads your location by GPS, and switches your home to normal, when your are heading home, with a door sensor that does the same, when someone enters,  and use water heaters to only work when needed in the afternoon, use tankless water heaters to heat sink water as needed, not heating 40-80 gallons , and keeping it hot,  heat the water around 5-8pm then not again until the next day, say power on to the water heater  from 5-6pm to around 10-11pm, then off unless needed, that along with extra insulation, and point of use water heaters, using 2-3kw for 30sec to a minute or so, a couple times, is more efficient than keeping 80 gallons hot all day,  add a solar thermal water heater with this, and a heat exchanger, to maintain hot water, neither systems would be used saving power, the sun heating water to about 200-250°f heating the stored water to about 140°, before starting to heat a secondary heat  dump tank, to supply heat for the home, or just extra heat for the water heater tank, or possibly just a heat dump to prevent the system from over heating, and dump into a external radiator if that overheats,   fail safes are important!!  If most homes and buildings had a similar system like this, there would be Soo much less energy used, we are so used to cheap energy , and just leaving everything running,  we are getting into trouble now that energy is costing more! It is easy to build solar thermal collectors,  and sand holds heat very well,  a copper coil of tubing in sand, heated by solar heat the sand to 300-400°f with concentrated solar,  can possibly supply heat all night! Especially if heating water stored in a tank, 
Sorry for all the rambling, 
I hope to build a large reflector to heat about 100-209 lbs of sand to heat water, then use a secondary reflector to heat a small portion of the water to make steam, to do the work of a 4hp gas engine, for about 4 hours a day, to generate power for my off grid home, to charge batteries,  and use a wood stove to do the same in rainy days and in winter!  It's going to be a complex system, and take time, but I hope to power my home, and maybe a vehicle with with sun and wood and paper scraps, maybe used vegoil!

author: @gameoverwehaveeverypixelco1258
text:
You can buy flourescent 3d printing spools, make a grooved light Guide, thin layer to put on panel, test in daylight.
Or make own using a soft drink bottle, there's are tools to make it cut into thin strips for 3d printing machine.

author: @BearerOfLightSonOfGod
text: Note to self: Heat destroys the solar panels and aerogel stops most heat cyrogels are similar to aerogels and can do the same thing but are easier to make. The compound made in the video can be turned into a cryogel preventing heat buildup possibly. Look into this concept later.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Imagine a Florescent cryogel  at work inside a HydroPanel?

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 Interesting idea. Another thing to add to the list... does it cool enough to be worth doing?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 of course! And it would contribute to the water collection as well.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @captainnemo6655 I am not sure about the "water" collection benefit. Presumably the gel would contain a chemical ?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 nope, no chemicals needed, just the curious effect of dew point moisture collection. Have you seen the DIY videos of the HydroPanels on YouTube? Also Rob has a video on making this kind of machine already.

author: @gameoverwehaveeverypixelco1258
text:
Can you make it using resin.
Yes doing it on a frenel lens will make lots of little light guide groves.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - but you would need a solvent soluble dye the stuff used in highlighters is water soluble

author: @keithianlocke
text:
The trick is going to be how to utilise this over an entire solar panel.
What about a grid of fluorescent "rods" which allow light to pass through, as well as "charging" the fluorescence?

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: Really really good idea... Also have you ever considered their use in HydroPanels? You could use a Florescent Cryogel that can do double duty as turbo charger for the solar cells and aid in water collection via condensation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am with Captain memo on this mate - that is a really really good idea

replies.author: @keithianlocke
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering columbia University, along with Cambridge, have come up with a way to convert infrared to visible spectrum, but can't see how it could work cheaply for solar.

author: @dans-designs
text:
I love it!
I tried a similar idea a few years ago:
small 3.3v solar panels with Lit (a glow in the dark powder branded as brightest in the world) attached to the sticky side of see-thru cellotape, multiple sheets sandwiched between 2 panels with around 12 small 2v UV LEDs shinning into the stack of "glowtape" from all 4 sides (not the backs of the solar panels).. unfortunately the LEDs weren't bright enough to fluoresce the whole stack but it did make some voltage..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is interesting mate - I have ordered some LIT

replies.author: @dans-designs
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering ah cool, looking forward to your results!!

replies.author: @GEOsustainable
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I can't wait to see that. I have ordered it too.

author: @custos3249
text: Intriguing - makes me wonder if uranium glass would work/have better properties

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: I was just thinking the same too... And if Uranium Glass works, can a whole panel of uranium glass be turned into a solar cell?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Just thinking of the waste disposal nightmare that would be ...

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @DFPercush why waste? When it came be made to last for decades by fully unlocking the solar cells potential to generate electricity day & night in addition to being able to generate water and fuels.

replies.author: @custos3249
replies.text: @DFPercush Not really. Though not made anymore, decorative uranium glass is still widely bought and sold. Once trapped in glass, the oxide is pretty much inert so long as you're not grinding the glass into fine powder.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @custos3249 this is what I was thinking of making it with. Panels of decorative Uranium glass. Can Uranium glass be made Flourescent as well?

author: @AdricM
text: Yes,  ive done a number of projects collecting the fluricene from hilighters.    fyi the hydrogel that orbees/water marbles etc etc will absorb the juice too..  interestingly (for those who have not seen fluorocene powder its brown/red when dried/concentrated.  for anyone looking for ways to dye oil based paints or whatnot with florescent  green/yellow the powder does not do well from hilighters.  but you can buy Airconditioner leak check  oil pretty cheaply. and it glows wonderfully.   Cheers rob,  i figgured you were working towards this Robart.    i suspect just the markers on the edge of the acrylic light guide panels might work too.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suspect you are right mate and really nice adice and help you posted there - thanks for taking the time to do that

author: @stevetobias4890
text: So basically you could paint it onto glass or plastic glass (acrylic) as you show could save you a lot of money instead of buying flourescent acrylic.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah - but it is jelly mate so there are going to be a lot of long term issues - drying out, rot, insects - but it can be used for experimentation and I suspect a lot getting ths idea to work practically is going to be about experimenting with the geometry - for example removing the need for tracking

author: @-COBRA
text: Interesting that covering the solar panel makes it produce more electricity.

replies.author: @AdricM
replies.text: He is side lighting it.  so now the cover is catching the side rays.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: its the larger surface area for trapping/converting the localised light ,with the free bonus of cooling the cell

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 just like you can do inside of a working HydroPanel :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's more of a light guide mate as opposed to a cover - what's on my mind is how well it work from different angles - if it does work well from different angles we might be able to do away with mechanical solar tracking systems - that would be quite a saving

author: @stopit4uca
text: Could probably pour it between two panes of glass

replies.author: @-COBRA
replies.text: it will dissolve by sun heat

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: You would have to do both outside surfaces as well as the centre to increase the surface area of projection onto the solar panel

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @-COBRA it does not have to be gelatin... There are other jelly materials that can be used

replies.author: @stopit4uca
replies.text: @cobra yes you're correct evaporation will occur but as it does the gelatin will become a thin layer staining the glass as well as someone pointed out there is other gels that are heat resistant even clear silicone the UV Inc could probably be used in between.

replies.author: @captainnemo6655
replies.text: @stopit4uca Florescent Silicone gel sealant, like the kind used in aquariums glass.
